turn_a: What is the exact time?
turn_b: The dawn is breaking.

turn_a: The dawn is breaking.
turn_b: I wonder that the keeper of the prison would let you in.

turn_a: I wonder that the keeper of the prison would let you in.
turn_b: He knows me because I often come, Socrates; moreover. I have done him a kindness.

turn_a: He knows me because I often come, Socrates; moreover. I have done him a kindness.
turn_b: And are you only just arrived?

turn_a: And are you only just arrived?
turn_b: No, I came some time ago.

turn_a: No, I came some time ago.
turn_b: Then why did you sit and say nothing, instead of at once awakening me?

turn_a: Then why did you sit and say nothing, instead of at once awakening me?
turn_b: I should not have liked myself, Socrates, to be in such great trouble and unrest as you are--indeed I should not: I have been watching with amazement your peaceful slumbers; and for that reason I did not awake you, because I wished to minimize the pain. I have always thought you to be of a happy disposition; but never did I see anything like the easy, tranquil manner in which you bear this calamity.

turn_a: I should not have liked myself, Socrates, to be in such great trouble and unrest as you are--indeed I should not: I have been watching with amazement your peaceful slumbers; and for that reason I did not awake you, because I wished to minimize the pain. I have always thought you to be of a happy disposition; but never did I see anything like the easy, tranquil manner in which you bear this calamity.
turn_b: Why, Crito, when a man has reached my age he ought not to be repining at the approach of death.

turn_a: Why, Crito, when a man has reached my age he ought not to be repining at the approach of death.
turn_b: And yet other old men find themselves in similar misfortunes, and age does not prevent them from repining.

turn_a: And yet other old men find themselves in similar misfortunes, and age does not prevent them from repining.
turn_b: That is true. But you have not told me why you come at this early hour.

turn_a: That is true. But you have not told me why you come at this early hour.
turn_b: I come to bring you a message which is sad and painful; not, as I believe, to yourself, but to all of us who are your friends, and saddest of all to me.

turn_a: I come to bring you a message which is sad and painful; not, as I believe, to yourself, but to all of us who are your friends, and saddest of all to me.
turn_b: What? Has the ship come from Delos, on the arrival of which I am to die?

turn_a: What? Has the ship come from Delos, on the arrival of which I am to die?
turn_b: No, the ship has not actually arrived, but she will probably be here to-day, as persons who have come from Sunium tell me that they have left her there; and therefore to-morrow, Socrates, will be the last day of your life.

turn_a: No, the ship has not actually arrived, but she will probably be here to-day, as persons who have come from Sunium tell me that they have left her there; and therefore to-morrow, Socrates, will be the last day of your life.
turn_b: Very well, Crito; if such is the will of God, I am willing; but my belief is that there will be a delay of a day.

turn_a: Very well, Crito; if such is the will of God, I am willing; but my belief is that there will be a delay of a day.
turn_b: Why do you think so?

turn_a: Why do you think so?
turn_b: I will tell you. I am to die on the day after the arrival of the ship?

turn_a: I will tell you. I am to die on the day after the arrival of the ship?
turn_b: Yes; that is what the authorities say.

turn_a: Yes; that is what the authorities say.
turn_b: But I do not think that the ship will be here until to-morrow; this I infer from a vision which I had last night, or rather only just now, when you fortunately allowed me to sleep.

turn_a: But I do not think that the ship will be here until to-morrow; this I infer from a vision which I had last night, or rather only just now, when you fortunately allowed me to sleep.
turn_b: And what was the nature of the vision?

turn_a: And what was the nature of the vision?
turn_b: There appeared to me the likeness of a woman, fair and comely, clothed in bright raiment, who called to me and said: O Socrates, 'The third day hence to fertile Phthia shalt thou go.' (Homer, Il.)

turn_a: There appeared to me the likeness of a woman, fair and comely, clothed in bright raiment, who called to me and said: O Socrates, 'The third day hence to fertile Phthia shalt thou go.' (Homer, Il.)
turn_b: What a singular dream, Socrates!

turn_a: What a singular dream, Socrates!
turn_b: There can be no doubt about the meaning, Crito, I think.

turn_a: There can be no doubt about the meaning, Crito, I think.
turn_b: Yes; the meaning is only too clear. But, oh! my beloved Socrates, let me entreat you once more to take my advice and escape. For if you die I shall not only lose a friend who can never be replaced, but there is another evil: people who do not know you and me will believe that I might have saved you if I had been willing to give money, but that I did not care. Now, can there be a worse disgrace than this--that I should be thought to value money more than the life of a friend? For the many will not be persuaded that I wanted you to escape, and that you refused.

turn_a: Yes; the meaning is only too clear. But, oh! my beloved Socrates, let me entreat you once more to take my advice and escape. For if you die I shall not only lose a friend who can never be replaced, but there is another evil: people who do not know you and me will believe that I might have saved you if I had been willing to give money, but that I did not care. Now, can there be a worse disgrace than this--that I should be thought to value money more than the life of a friend? For the many will not be persuaded that I wanted you to escape, and that you refused.
turn_b: But why, my dear Crito, should we care about the opinion of the many? Good men, and they are the only persons who are worth considering, will think of these things truly as they occurred.

turn_a: But why, my dear Crito, should we care about the opinion of the many? Good men, and they are the only persons who are worth considering, will think of these things truly as they occurred.
turn_b: But you see, Socrates, that the opinion of the many must be regarded, for what is now happening shows that they can do the greatest evil to any one who has lost their good opinion.

turn_a: But you see, Socrates, that the opinion of the many must be regarded, for what is now happening shows that they can do the greatest evil to any one who has lost their good opinion.
turn_b: I only wish it were so, Crito; and that the many could do the greatest evil; for then they would also be able to do the greatest good-- and what a fine thing this would be! But in reality they can do neither; for they cannot make a man either wise or foolish; and whatever they do is the result of chance.

turn_a: I only wish it were so, Crito; and that the many could do the greatest evil; for then they would also be able to do the greatest good-- and what a fine thing this would be! But in reality they can do neither; for they cannot make a man either wise or foolish; and whatever they do is the result of chance.
turn_b: Well, I will not dispute with you; but please to tell me, Socrates, whether you are not acting out of regard to me and your other friends: are you not afraid that if you escape from prison we may get into trouble with the informers for having stolen you away, and lose either the whole or a great part of our property; or that even a worse evil may happen to us? Now, if you fear on our account, be at ease; for in order to save you, we ought surely to run this, or even a greater risk; be persuaded, then, and do as I say.

turn_a: Well, I will not dispute with you; but please to tell me, Socrates, whether you are not acting out of regard to me and your other friends: are you not afraid that if you escape from prison we may get into trouble with the informers for having stolen you away, and lose either the whole or a great part of our property; or that even a worse evil may happen to us? Now, if you fear on our account, be at ease; for in order to save you, we ought surely to run this, or even a greater risk; be persuaded, then, and do as I say.
turn_b: Yes, Crito, that is one fear which you mention, but by no means the only one.

turn_a: And what will the evil be, whither tending and what affecting, in the disobedient person?
turn_b: Clearly, affecting the body; that is what is destroyed by the evil.

turn_a: Clearly, affecting the body; that is what is destroyed by the evil.
turn_b: Very good; and is not this true, Crito, of other things which we need not separately enumerate? In questions of just and unjust, fair and foul, good and evil, which are the subjects of our present consultation, ought we to follow the opinion of the many and to fear them; or the opinion of the one man who has understanding? ought we not to fear and reverence him more than all the rest of the world: and if we desert him shall we not destroy and injure that principle in us which may be assumed to be improved by justice and deteriorated by injustice;--there is such a principle?

turn_a: Very good; and is not this true, Crito, of other things which we need not separately enumerate? In questions of just and unjust, fair and foul, good and evil, which are the subjects of our present consultation, ought we to follow the opinion of the many and to fear them; or the opinion of the one man who has understanding? ought we not to fear and reverence him more than all the rest of the world: and if we desert him shall we not destroy and injure that principle in us which may be assumed to be improved by justice and deteriorated by injustice;--there is such a principle?
turn_b: Certainly there is, Socrates.

turn_a: Certainly there is, Socrates.
turn_b: Take a parallel instance:--if, acting under the advice of those who have no understanding, we destroy that which is improved by health and is deteriorated by disease, would life be worth having? And that which has been destroyed is--the body?

turn_a: Then, my friend, we must not regard what the many say of us: but what he, the one man who has understanding of just and unjust, will say, and what the truth will say. And therefore you begin in error when you advise that we should regard the opinion of the many about just and unjust, good and evil, honorable and dishonorable.--'Well,' some one will say, 'but the many can kill us.'
turn_b: Yes, Socrates; that will clearly be the answer.

turn_a: Yes, Socrates; that will clearly be the answer.
turn_b: And it is true; but still I find with surprise that the old argument is unshaken as ever. And I should like to know whether I may say the same of another proposition--that not life, but a good life, is to be chiefly valued?

turn_a: And it is true; but still I find with surprise that the old argument is unshaken as ever. And I should like to know whether I may say the same of another proposition--that not life, but a good life, is to be chiefly valued?
turn_b: Yes, that also remains unshaken.

turn_a: Yes, that also remains unshaken.
turn_b: And a good life is equivalent to a just and honorable one--that holds also?

turn_a: I think that you are right, Socrates; how then shall we proceed?
turn_b: Let us consider the matter together, and do you either refute me if you can, and I will be convinced; or else cease, my dear friend, from repeating to me that I ought to escape against the wishes of the Athenians: for I highly value your attempts to persuade me to do so, but I may not be persuaded against my own better judgment. And now please to consider my first position, and try how you can best answer me.

turn_a: Again, Crito, may we do evil?
turn_b: Surely not, Socrates.

turn_a: Surely not, Socrates.
turn_b: And what of doing evil in return for evil, which is the morality of the many--is that just or not?

turn_a: You may proceed, for I have not changed my mind.
turn_b: Then I will go on to the next point, which may be put in the form of a question:--Ought a man to do what he admits to be right, or ought he to betray the right?

turn_a: Then I will go on to the next point, which may be put in the form of a question:--Ought a man to do what he admits to be right, or ought he to betray the right?
turn_b: He ought to do what he thinks right.

turn_a: He ought to do what he thinks right.
turn_b: But if this is true, what is the application? In leaving the prison against the will of the Athenians, do I wrong any? or rather do I not wrong those whom I ought least to wrong? Do I not desert the principles which were acknowledged by us to be just--what do you say?

turn_a: But if this is true, what is the application? In leaving the prison against the will of the Athenians, do I wrong any? or rather do I not wrong those whom I ought least to wrong? Do I not desert the principles which were acknowledged by us to be just--what do you say?
turn_b: I cannot tell, Socrates, for I do not know.

turn_a: I have nothing to say, Socrates.
turn_b: Leave me then, Crito, to fulfil the will of God, and to follow whither he leads.
